### Post by ski309 on 2007-03-08
Hi all.  I've tried multiple times to get my NTFS partition working under Linux, but it won't show up.  The weird thing is it showed up once or twice but after that it was gone.

I'm on a Dell E1405 laptop.  My fstab:

```
# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sda3 :
UUID=80d86f3d-b599-4417-aabd-723c6302b73f / ext3 defaults,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sda5 :
UUID=68d1a496-6290-4ac4-8e77-b4cb49abe9b5 none swap sw 0 0
/dev/scd0 /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto 0 0
/dev/sda2 /media/windows ntfs-3g defaults,nls=utf8,umask=007,gid=46 0 1

```
If you need any other information, let me know.

 sudo mount -a

### Post by ski309 on 2007-03-09
I got an error message when I tried **sudo mount -a**. It said I probably hadn't closed Windows properly, which makes sense since I normally just put the laptop's screen down so it goes into standby/hibernate.  I closed Windows properly before going to bed last night, I'll let you know if that fixed things.

If that does fix things, here's another question:  The error message stated I could make the partition read only through another driver or something like that.  Can I setup Ubuntu to make the NTFS read only if it can't make the drive r/w?

---

### Post by ski309 on 2007-03-09
Closing Windows properly fixed it.  Thanks a lot?  Is there a way to setup Ubuntu to make the NTFS read only if it can't make the drive r/w?

---

### Post by taurus on 2007-03-09
Modify your /etc/fstab

```
gksudo gedit /etc/fstab
```
and change the last line to look like this.

```
/dev/sda2   /media/windows   ntfs    nls=utf8,umask=0222   0   0
```

---
